The wi_skills agent correctly identified the methodology (HSC root, DPT) but the implementation was flawed, producing an effectively empty trajectory. utility_details.judge_error: Both runs failed the V3 verifier check (test_v3_paga_key_in_uns), meaning neither run stored a formal PAGA connectivity key in adata.uns. The wi_skills run explicitly acknowledged that PAGA/DPT was unstable and fell back to a PCA-projection-based pseudotime. The wo_skills run similarly could not run PAGA successfully and used an annotation-guided hierarchy instead. Neither agent produced a true sc.tl.paga graph stored in adata.uns['paga'].
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills achieved similar scores across all six evaluation items, both successfully reconstructing a biologically coherent hematopoietic hierarchy rooted at HSC with CMP as branch point, pseudotime ordering, and gene dynamics along each lineage. However, wi_skills shows slight advantages: (1) it invoked the scanpy skill and completed the task in only 6 steps vs 22 steps for wo_skills, demonstrating much higher efficiency; (2) the wi_skills report is more concise and focused; (3) wi_skills produced 5 figures including gene trend figures for both lineages, while wo_skills also produced 6 figures. The wo_skills report is actually more detailed (4570 bytes vs 2431 bytes) with explicit FDR values and more genes listed. Critically, neither run produced a formal PAGA graph in adata.uns, which is the key methodology described in the expected results. Overall, wi_skills is more efficient (fewer steps, uses the skill as instructed), while wo_skills produced a slightly more detailed report. In terms of biological correctness and task completion, they are roughly equivalent, but wi_skills is preferred for its efficiency and adherence to the /scanpy skill invocation requirement.
